messages.content:
Using the following criteria:ENGLISH LEARNING EXPERIENCE (Use prose with coherent paragraphs. Don’t use bullet points.) o How long has the student been learning English? 
o How was their previous learning organised? (type of courses/schools/private tutorials/ online or face-to-face/ group or  individual / mono- or multilingual: describe 1 or 2 most important or most recent experiences) 
o What did he/she (or his/her previous teachers) focus on? (grammar/lexis/pronunciation/receptive skills/productive  skills/exam preparation/conversational English – refer to each stage mentioned above) 
o What methods were used by the teachers? (be specific and refer to each stage mentioned above: How did the student  learn new language structures? How did they practice them? How did they develop their receptive and productive skills?) o Which of these methods were useful/engaging/effective – in the student’s opinion? Why? 
o Which of these methods did NOT work – in their opinion? Why? : Answer for a B2 student with the following experience:Online classes, some individual when learning grammar, mostly group classes.

The student has been learning English for around 5-6 years now. Their previous learning experience has mostly been organised through online classes, with occasional individual tutorials for grammar and mostly group classes. The student's most recent experience was with a group class where they also had access to online lessons and resources.

In terms of focus, the student's previous teachers have mostly zeroed in on grammar, lexis, and receptive and productive skills. Exam preparation has also been a part of the curriculum. As for the methods used, the teachers opted for a variety of approaches for each stage of learning. For example, for learning new language structures, the teachers used interactive whiteboards, online activities, and video presentations. Classroom discussions and feedback sessions were used for practicing and developing receptive and productive skills. Exam preparation was done through mock tests and practical exercises.

In the student's opinion, the most effective and engaging method was the use of interactive whiteboards, which made it easier to understand new language structures and concepts. Additionally, classroom discussions and feedback sessions provided valuable insights into the nuances of the language, enhancing the student's receptive and productive skills. On the other hand, merely focusing on grammar made the learning experience monotonous and boring for the student.

Overall, the student has had a positive experience learning English online with group classes. The use of interactive whiteboards and classroom discussions has been useful in enhancing their learning experience.

Using the following criteriaREASONS TO STUDY ENGLISH: 
o Why is the student learning English now? 
o Is their motivation extrinsic or intrinsic? Why? Define the motivation type. Explain with examples. 
o How does this motivation help the student learn? What extra incentives might the student need to learn effectively?   
LEARNER EXPECTATIONS:  
o What does the student want to focus on during a language course? 
(Speaking? Writing? Listening? Reading? Grammar? Vocabulary? Functional/Situational language? Pronunciation?) o What does the student expect from the teacher in each of these areas: 
● topics for the lessons? 
● resources prepared by the teacher? 
● clarification of new grammar and vocabulary? 
● practice of language – accuracy vs fluency? 
● practice of skills – receptive vs productive? 
● amount of Student Talk Time in the classroom? 
● feedback on language and error correction? 
o What activities does the student want to do in the classroom? (Focus on activity types and interaction patterns) : for a B2 student with the following answers:MOTIVATION TO LEARN ENGLISH (Use prose with coherent paragraphs. Don’t use bullet points.) REASONS TO STUDY ENGLISH: Communication while travel and for conversation with others
LEARNER EXPECTATIONS:  To speak fluently in conversation using proper English

The student's primary motivation for learning English is clear communication while traveling and conversing with others. Their motivation seems to be intrinsic, meaning it comes from within the individual, rather than extrinsic, which would be motivated by external factors like praise or rewards. The student likely values being able to travel and converse with others, and views learning proper English as a necessary skill to achieve those goals.

This intrinsic motivation can be helpful for the student in achieving their language learning goals, as it allows them to immerse themselves in the language without feeling like it's a chore. However, extra incentives like tangible rewards or recognition may still be needed to keep them motivated and engaged in their learning.

In terms of learner expectations, the student wants to focus on speaking fluently in conversation using proper English. They likely want to work on their speaking skills and be able to hold conversations with ease, so that they can communicate effectively while traveling or talking with others.

From the teacher, the student expects topics that are relevant to conversational situations. They would likely benefit from resources that are specifically designed for improving their speaking skills and their vocabulary. Clarification of new grammar and vocabulary to improve their fluency in conversation is also a priority, along with accuracy in their language use.

The student seems to be interested in practicing both receptive and productive skills, with an emphasis on fluency over accuracy. They would likely benefit from classroom activities that are open-ended and encourage experimentation and risk-taking, like role-playing or debates. Feedback and error correction are important for improving student's language skills throughout the course of their learning. The student would like an ample amount of Student Talk Time in the classroom, as they place importance on being able to practice and develop their speaking skills in a supportive environment.

Overall, the student has set goals for speaking fluently in conversation using proper English. Their expectations from the teacher are clear and targeted at achieving that goal, with an emphasis on activities that encourage communication and error correction.

According to Honey & Mumford's inventory, the student's strongest learning preference is pragmatic. This suggests that they prefer to learn by doing and translating theory into practical action. The student is likely motivated by real-world applications of learning and enjoys problem-solving.

In terms of language activities, the student would likely be engaged in activities that have practical applications, such as role-playing in real-life situations or using language to complete tasks. They may also enjoy activities that allow them to apply their knowledge and skills to a specific goal, such as creating a presentation or giving a speech.

To engage the student fully in the experiential learning cycle, the teacher will need to incorporate activities that represent the other three learning preferences. For example, for a student with a reflective learning preference, the teacher could assign reflective writing tasks or journaling exercises. For an activist learning preference, the teacher could introduce language games or debates that encourage participation and physical movement. Finally, for a student with a theoretical learning preference, the teacher could incorporate more structured grammar or vocabulary exercises.

Incorporating activities for all four learning preferences is important to ensure that the student is engaged and fully invested in the learning process. By providing a variety of activities that cater to different learning styles, the teacher can create a more inclusive learning environment that addresses the needs and preferences of all students. This can lead to a more effective and enjoyable learning experience overall.

Format this reference :Swan & Walter The Good Grammar Book p 60 & 292: Using this formatting:Grellet, F. (2010) Developing Reading Skills: A Practical Guide to Reading Comprehension Exercises. Cambridge:  Cambridge University Press.
Swan, M. & Walter, C. (2014) The Good Grammar Book. Oxford: Oxford University Press.
APA style for:Swan & Walter The Good Grammar Book p 60 & 292
Swan, M. & Walter, C. (2014). The good grammar book (pp. 60, 292). Oxford, UK: Oxford University Press.

Format this reference :Swan & Smith Learner English p 166: Using this formatting:Grellet, F. (2010) Developing Reading Skills: A Practical Guide to Reading Comprehension Exercises. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.
Swan, M. & Smith, B. (2001). Learner English: A teacher's guide to interference and other problems. Cambridge, UK: Cambridge University Press. (p. 166)

Format this reference :Scrivener Teaching Grammar p 166-168 Using this formatting:Grellet, F. (2010) Developing Reading Skills: A Practical Guide to Reading Comprehension Exercises. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.
Scrivener, J. (2010). Teaching grammar: A guide for teachers. Oxford, UK: Macmillan Education. (pp. 166-168).
APA formatting: Teaching English Grammar What to teach and how to teach it, Jim Scrivener pp166-168
Scrivener, J. (2010). Teaching English grammar: What to teach and how to teach it (pp. 166-168). Oxford, UK: Macmillan Education.
APA for: Swan & Smith: Learner English Second Edition A teacher's guide to interference and other problems  p 166
Swan, M., & Smith, B. (2001). Learner English: A teacher's guide to interference and other problems (2nd ed., p. 166). Cambridge, UK: Cambridge University Press.

http://www.xroads.com/~tcs/hotdog/hotdog.html: for  Sandy Millin Typical Problems for Polish learners of English posted on October 27, 2018
